skill.name: chemical-storage-sorter
skill.description: Sort laboratory chemicals into safe storage groups by hazard classification (acids, bases, oxidizers, flammables, toxics). Identifies incompatible pairs, generates storage plans with warnings, and supports OSHA/NFPA compliance for lab safety.
skill.summary: Automatically classify and sort laboratory chemicals into safe storage groups based on hazard compatibility.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 44 static analysis findings across 3 files (549 lines). All findings are false positives: chemical formulas (HCl, H2SO4, H2O2) triggered weak-cryptographic-algorithm matches, markdown fenced code blocks were misidentified as Ruby shell backtick execution, and documentation reference URLs (GitHub source, OSHA guidelines) were flagged as suspicious network activity. The Python script implements a straightforward chemical classifier with no obfuscation, credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection. The skill functions as documented: it classifies chemicals into hazard groups and generates storage plans.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (False Positive)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged chemical formulas (H2SO4, HNO3, HCl) present in COMPATIBILITY_GROUPS example lists as weak cryptographic algorithm names. The code performs no cryptographic operations. Chemical formulas are legitimate data values for a chemistry classification tool.

medium_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown fenced code blocks (triple backticks with bash/python/text labels) as Ruby backtick command execution. These are documentation examples showing CLI usage patterns and Python code snippets for human readers. The skill does legitimately invoke python scripts/main.py, but this is its intended core function with no injection risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged two URLs in SKILL.md documentation: a GitHub source repository link (line 7) and an OSHA chemical storage guidelines reference link (line 162). Both are legitimate documentation citations, not suspicious network endpoints. The Python script does not make any network calls.

content.user_title: Sort Laboratory Chemicals for Safe Storage
content.value_statement: Laboratory chemical storage requires careful hazard classification to prevent dangerous reactions. This skill automatically categorizes chemicals into safe storage groups and identifies incompatible pairs to prevent accidents.
actual_capabilities: Classify chemicals into 6 hazard groups: acids, bases, oxidizers, flammables, toxics, and general
Check compatibility between any two chemicals and warn about incompatible pairs
Generate a complete storage plan with group assignments and incompatibility warnings
Accept chemical names, IUPAC names, and CAS numbers as input
Flag multi-hazard chemicals and assign them to the most restrictive storage group
Provide OSHA and NFPA aligned segregation guidelines for each storage group
limitations: Chemical classification uses keyword matching, not a comprehensive chemical database
Cannot interpret Safety Data Sheets or provide toxicology advice
Does not verify actual inventory or physical storage conditions in your lab
Multi-hazard chemicals require manual confirmation of the assigned storage group

use_cases.title: Lab Manager Creates Storage Plan
use_cases.description: A laboratory manager provides a list of 50 chemicals and receives a sorted storage plan with group assignments and incompatibility warnings for each group.

use_cases.title: Researcher Checks Compatibility
use_cases.description: A researcher planning a new experiment checks whether two chemicals can be stored in the same cabinet before reorganizing their workspace.

use_cases.title: Safety Officer Audits Inventory
use_cases.description: An institutional safety officer reviews an existing chemical inventory to identify potential storage violations and generate a compliance report.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Chemical Sort
prompt_templates.prompt: Sort these chemicals into compatible storage groups: HCl, NaOH, ethanol, H2O2, sucrose, KMnO4.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick classification of a short chemical list into hazard groups

prompt_templates.title: Compatibility Check Between Two Chemicals
prompt_templates.prompt: Check if sodium hydroxide and nitric acid are compatible for storage. If not, explain the risk.
prompt_templates.scenario: Verify safe co-storage before placing chemicals in the same cabinet

prompt_templates.title: Full Lab Reorganization
prompt_templates.prompt: I am reorganizing my lab and need a complete storage plan for these chemicals: hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, ethanol, acetone, hydrogen peroxide, potassium permanganate, sodium chloride, PBS buffer, formalin, ethyl acetate, methanol, sulfuric acid, ammonia solution, hexane, glycerol. Show the storage groups and highlight all incompatibility warnings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive lab reorganization with multiple chemical types

prompt_templates.title: Audit Existing Inventory for Violations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this chemical inventory list for potential storage violations: [paste your inventory list]. Group the chemicals by hazard class and flag any incompatible pairs that should not be stored together.
prompt_templates.scenario: Safety audit of an existing lab inventory to identify risks

output_examples.input: Sort these chemicals: HCl, NaOH, ethanol, H2O2
output:
ACIDS STORAGE:
  • HCl
  ⚠️ Keep away from: bases, oxidizers, cyanides, sulfides

BASES STORAGE:
  • NaOH
  ⚠️ Keep away from: acids, oxidizers, halogenated

FLAMMABLES STORAGE:
  • ethanol
  ⚠️ Keep away from: oxidizers, acids

OXIDIZERS STORAGE:
  • H2O2
  ⚠️ Keep away from: flammables, acids, bases, reducing

output_examples.input: Check compatibility between hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide
output_examples.output: INCOMPATIBLE: acids cannot be stored with bases. These chemicals should be kept in separate cabinets at least 3 feet apart. Acids require a corrosion-resistant acid cabinet with secondary containment. Bases require a dedicated base cabinet.

best_practices: Always verify storage plans against official Safety Data Sheets for each chemical before implementing changes
Use standard IUPAC names or CAS numbers for best classification accuracy with the keyword matching system
Run a compatibility check after adding any new chemical to an existing storage group
anti_patterns: Storing chemicals in alphabetical order without considering hazard group compatibility
Assuming dilute acids and bases are safe to store together with other groups
Ignoring multi-hazard chemicals that belong to more than one hazard classification

faq.question: What types of chemical names does this skill accept?
faq.answer: It accepts standard IUPAC names, common names, and CAS numbers. The system uses keyword matching against chemical names in its database.

faq.question: Can this skill replace an official lab safety audit?
faq.answer: No. This skill is a first-check tool only. Always verify storage plans with official Safety Data Sheets and your institutional safety officer.

faq.question: Does this skill access an external chemical database?
faq.answer: No. All classifications are done locally using built-in keyword matching. No network requests are made by the Python script.

faq.question: How does the skill handle chemicals in multiple hazard groups?
faq.answer: Multi-hazard chemicals are automatically assigned to the most restrictive applicable group. Manual review is recommended for confirmation.

faq.question: What are the main storage groups available?
faq.answer: The six groups are acids, bases, oxidizers, flammables, toxics, and general. Each group has specific storage requirements and incompatibility warnings.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with a pre-existing chemical inventory spreadsheet?
faq.answer: Yes. Paste comma-separated or line-separated chemical names from your inventory list. The skill will sort all chemicals into their appropriate storage groups.
